statementLines: As per the Constitution of India, every High Court shall be a court of record. What does it signify?
I. The decisions and judgements of a High Court are recognized as legal precedents in any other lower court in India.
II. A High Court has the power to punish for its own contempt.
III. A High Court can withdraw a case pending in a subordinate court under its jurisdiction, if it involves a substantial question of law that requires interpretation of the Constitution of India.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: As per the Constitution of India, every High Court shall be a court of record. What does it signify? I. The decisions and judgements of a High Court are recognized as legal precedents in any other lower court in India. II. A High Court has the power to punish for its own contempt. III. A High Court can withdraw a case pending in a subordinate court under its jurisdiction, if it involves a substantial question of law that requires interpretation of the Constitution of India. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Article 215 of the Indian Constitution provides that every High Court shall be a court of record and shall have all the powers of such a court including the power to punish for contempt of itself.**Statements I and II are correct. **As a Court of record, every High Court has two main powers: The judgements, proceedings and acts of the High Court are recorded for perpetual memory and testimony.**These records and judgements have evidentiary value **and**are recognized as legal precedents**. These cannot be questioned when produced before any subordinate court. (Recently, the Supreme Court has reiterated that High Courts are Courts of Record and that whatever is recorded in their proceedings is presumed to be correct and cannot be contradicted later by parties or counsel.) The** High Court has the power to punish for contempt of court,**not only for itself but also for other High courts, subordinate courts and tribunals.**

Statement III is incorrect. As per Article 228 of the Constitution of India: If the High Court is satisfied that a case **pending in a court subordinate to it involves a substantial question of law as to the interpretation of this **Constitution the determination of which is necessary for the disposal of the case, it shall withdraw the case **and may
- (a) either dispose of the case itself, or
(b) determine the said question of law and return the case to the court from which the case has been so withdrawn together with a copy of its judgment on such question, and the said court shall on receipt thereof proceed to dispose of the case in conformity with such judgment.
This power is not sourced from the fact that High courts are a court of record under Article 215 of the Constitution of India.

The writ of prohibition can be issued only against judicial and quasi-judicial authorities.

questions.questionText: The Parliament of India may by law provide for the creation of All India Judicial Service if a resolution is passed declaring that it is necessary in the national interest to do so. The resolution to this effect: present and voting. present and voting.

options.a: Can be introduced in Lok Sabha only and shall be passed by a majority of its total membership.
options.b: Can be introduced in Lok Sabha only and shall be passed by a majority of not less than two-thirds of its members
options.c: Can be introduced in Rajya Sabha only and shall be passed by a majority of its total membership.
options.d: Can be introduced in Rajya Sabha only and shall be passed by a majority of not less than two-thirds of its members

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **Article 312 of the Constitution provides for the establishment of All India Judicial Service (AIJS). It empowers the Parliament to legislate for the creation of one or more All-India Services, including an All India Judicial Service. However, this can be done by the Parliament only** if the Rajya Sabha passes a resolution supported by not less than **two-thirds of the members present and voting, **declaring that it is necessary or expedient in the national interest to do so. Article 312 (1) provides that if the Rajya Sabha declares that it is necessary to create a service in national interest, the Parliament**"may by law provide for the creation of one or more all India services (including an all India judicial **service) **common to the Union and the States." Article 312 (4) provides that the law providing for the creation of an all-India judicial service may amend Chapter VI of Part VI of the Constitution of India.**But no such law shall be deemed to be an amendment of this Constitution for **the purposes of article 368. **Some provisions of the Constitution can be amended by a simple majority of the Parliament in the manner of ordinary legislative process. But these amendments do not come under Article 368.**The amendment under Article 312 would be by an ordinary law **as article 312(4) ensures that the law providing for the creation of an all-India judicial service may contain such provisions for the amendment of Chapter VI, Part VI.

They** were **incorporated in the Indian Constitution by the 42nd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1976. **Statement c is correct. **The provisions relating to Tribunals were introduced on the r**ecommendations of the Swaran **Singh Committee. **Statement d is correct.**To give effect to the provisions of Article 323A, the Parliament passed the Administrative Tribunals Act, 1985.**Under section 4 of the Administrative Tribunals Act of 1985, the Central Government is the **body empowered to establish administrative tribunals,both for the Union as well as for the States. **

statementLines: Consider the following disputes:
I. A conflict between the Union Government and a State Government regarding legislative powers.
II. A dispute between two States over the alignment of their common boundary.
III. An appeal against the Election Commission's order disqualifying a Member of Parliament. Which of the above fall under the original exclusive jurisdiction of the Supreme Court of India?
questions.questionText: Consider the following disputes: I. A conflict between the Union Government and a State Government regarding legislative powers. II. A dispute between two States over the alignment of their common boundary. III. An appeal against the Election Commission's order disqualifying a Member of Parliament. Which of the above fall under the original exclusive jurisdiction of the Supreme Court of India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **

Statement I is correct. Article 131 **of the Constitution gives the Supreme Court original and exclusive jurisdiction in disputes between the Centre and one or more States where the dispute involves legal rights.** A conflict over **legislative powers between the Union and a State falls within this jurisdiction. **

Statement II is correct. Boundary disputes between States are a concern of legal rights between States and are **exclusively heard by the Supreme Court under Article 131. **

Statement III is incorrect. **Appeals involving Election Commission orders regarding disqualification of a Member of Parliament** fall under the Supreme Court's appellate jurisdiction under Articles 136, 132-134, not under original **jurisdiction. **

statementLines: With reference to the National Legal Services Authority (NALSA), consider the following statements:
I. It has been constituted under the Legal Services Authorities Act, 1987 with one of the objectives to organize Lok Adalats for amicable settlement of disputes.

questions.questionText: With reference to the National Legal Services Authority (NALSA), consider the following statements: I. It has been constituted under the Legal Services Authorities Act, 1987 with one of the objectives to organize Lok Adalats for amicable settlement of disputes. II. The Chief Justice of India is the Patron-in-Chief of the National Legal Services Authority. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Article 39 A of the Constitution of India mentions that the State shall secure that the operation of the legal system **promotes justice, on a basis of equal opportunity, and shall, in particular, provide free legal aid,**by suitable legislation or schemes or in any other way, to ensure that opportunities for securing justice are not denied to any citizen by reason of economic or other disabilities. To fulfill this, the**Legal Services Authorities Act, 1987**was enacted and enforced on**9th November 1995**, creating a**nationwide network**to deliver**free and competent legal services**to the weaker sections of society.**

Statement I is correct:**The**National Legal Services Authority (NALSA) has been constituted under the Legal **Services Authorities Act, 1987**to provide free Legal Services to the weaker sections of the society and**to organize **Lok Adalats for amicable settlement of disputes. **

Statement II is correct:**According to section 3 of Legal Services Authorities Act, 1987, the Chief Justice of India **acts as Patron in Chief of **National Legal Services Authority (NALSA).

statementLines: Consider the following:

questions.questionText: Consider the following: I. National Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission (NCDRC) II. Central Information Commission (CIC) III. Election Commission of India (ECI) IV. Inter-State Council How many of the above are identified as quasi-judicial bodies in India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:**

Option c is correct answer ** Option I is correct:**The**National Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission (NCDRC)**is a**quasi-judicial body**established in**1988**under the**Consumer Protection Act, 1986**, with its headquarters in**New Delhi**. - It is headed by a**sitting or retired Supreme Court Judge**or a**Chief Justice of a High Court**, as per the**Tribunal **(Conditions of Service) Rules, 2021**. - Under**Section 21**of the Act, the NCDRC has jurisdiction over**consumer complaints valued above **2 crore** and also exercises **appellate and revisional powers** over orders from **State Commissions and District Forums**.
- As per **Section 23**, any person aggrieved by an order of the NCDRC may file an **appeal to the Supreme Court**
within **30 days**.

**Option II is correct:** The **Central Information Commission (CIC)** is a **quasi-judicial** body established in 2005 under the Right to Information Act, 2005, as an **independent statutory body** to promote **transparency and accountability** in government functioning.
**Option III is correct:** The Election Commission of India (ECI) is a permanent constitutional body established on 25th January 1950 under Article 324 of the Constitution. It is entrusted with the superintendence, direction, and control of elections to the Parliament, State Legislatures, and the offices of President and Vice-President of India, ensuring free and fair elections in the world's largest democracy. **The Commission, as a part of its quasi-judicial jurisdiction, settles disputes between the splinter groups of recognised parties.**Also, the Commission has the power to disqualify a candidate who has failed to lodge an account of his election expenses within the time and in the manner prescribed by law. The Commission has also the power for removing or reducing the period of such disqualification as also other disqualifications under the law.
**Option IV is incorrect: Inter-State Council**is constituted under article 263 of the constitution of India as an advisory and consultative body that serves to promote cooperation and coordination between the center and the states. It is **not a quasi-judicial** body.
Knowledge Base:The **Finance Commission of India** is a **quasi-judicial constitutional body** constituted by the **President every five years** or earlier under **Article 280** of the Constitution. Its main function is to make **recommendations to the President** on issues related to the **distribution of financial resources** between the Union and the States.
- It recommends the **distribution of the net proceeds of taxes** between the Centre and the States and also determines the **principles for grants-in-aid** to States from the **Consolidated Fund of India**. The Commission also suggests **measures to augment the Consolidated Fund of a State** to supplement the resources of **Panchayats and Municipalities**, based on the recommendations of the **State Finance Commissions**. Additionally, the President may refer **any other matter** to it in the interest of sound finance.

statementLines: In the context of the Indian judicial system, "Plea Bargaining" refers to:
questions.questionText: In the context of the Indian judicial system, "Plea Bargaining" refers to:

options.a: A process where the accused agrees to plead guilty in return for a lesser sentence or charge.
options.b: A process under which the accused seeks early release through remission or parole.
options.c: A mechanism allowing settlement of civil disputes through mediation or conciliation.
options.d: A procedure where the court reviews its own judgment under curative petition.

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Plea bargaining is a process in which a defendant in a criminal case agrees to plead guilty in exchange for some **concession from the prosecutor or the court**. It aims to resolve a criminal case without going to trial, thereby saving time, resources, and expenses for both the prosecution and the defendant.**Plea bargaining**was introduced in**2005**under**Chapter XXIA of the CrPC**to reduce case pendency. It allows an**accused to admit guilt in exchange for **leniency**in sentence. It applies only to**offences punishable up to 7 years**, excluding cases involving**women, children, **or socio-economic offences**.

The Nyayadhikari (presiding officer) of a Gram Nyayalaya is appointed by the State Government in consultation with the High Court of the State. II. Gram Nyayalaya has the jurisdiction to try both civil and criminal cases. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.**

Statement I is correct: **The**Gram Nyayalayas Act, 2008**was enacted to**provide access to justice at the grassroots **level**and to ensure that opportunities for securing justice are not denied due to**social, economic, or other **disabilities**. The Act aims to bring**speedy, inexpensive, and substantial justice**to rural citizens at their doorsteps, thereby implementing the**directive principle under Article 39A**of the Constitution, which mandates equal access to justice and free legal aid. According to the**Gram Nyayalayas Act, 2008**, the**Nyayadhikari**is appointed by the**State Government in **consultation with the High Court**. This provision ensures that appointments are made with judicial oversight.**

Statement II is correct: **A Gram Nyayalaya has jurisdiction to try**criminal cases specified in the First Schedule**and**civil suits, claims, or disputes specified in the Second Schedule**to the Act. Thus, its jurisdiction includes both**criminal cases**and** civil matters. **

options.d: Statement-I is incorrect but Statement-II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.**The Prisons Act 1894 is one of the oldest pieces of legislation in India dealing with laws enacted in relation to prisons in India.**

Statement I is correct: Prison is a State subject under List-II of the Seventh Schedule **in the Constitution. States have the primary role, responsibility and power to change the current prison laws, rules and regulations. Hence it is true that prisons are managed by the state governments with their own rules and regulations.**

Statement II is correct and is the correct explanation for statement I: **The management and administration of Prisons falls**exclusively in the domain of the State Governments and is governed by the Prisons Act, 1894 **and the Prison Manuals of the respective State Governments. Hence it is true that the Prisons Act 1894 kept the subject of prisons in the Provincial government (State governments) and**this explains why State governments have the power **to frame their own rules and regulations **regarding prison management.

statementLines: Consider the following- A person is a citizen of India and-
I. has for at least ten years held a judicial office in the territory of India.
II. in the opinion of the President, is a distinguished jurist.
III. has for at least ten years been an advocate of a High Court or of two or more such Courts in succession. As per the Constitution of India,
how many of the above persons shall be qualified for appointment as a Judge of a High Court in India?
questions.questionText: Consider the following- A person is a citizen of India and- I. has for at least ten years held a judicial office in the territory of India. II. in the opinion of the President, is a distinguished jurist. III. has for at least ten years been an advocate of a High Court or of two or more such Courts in succession. As per the Constitution of India, how many of the above persons shall be qualified for appointment as a Judge of a High Court in India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Option I and III are correct: **The qualifications for appointment as a Judge of a High Court are laid down in**Article **217(2**) of the Constitution of India. A**person shall not be qualified for appointment as a Judge of a High Court unless he/she:**1.Is a**citizen of India**, and 2.a) has**held **a**judicial office in **the** territory of India for at least ten years**, or 3.b) has been an**advocate of a High Court (**or of two or more such Courts in succession) for**at least ten years**.** Option II is incorrect: **The qualification of being a "**distinguished jurist in the opinion of the President**" applies**only **for appointment as a Judge of the Supreme Court**under**Article 124(3)**,**not**for **the**High Court**.
Therefore, only Options I and III correctly describe the constitutional qualifications for appointment as a Judge of a High Court.

statementLines: Consider the following pairs regarding Court/ Tribunals and their strength/composition: Court/Tribunal Authority Empowered to Determine its Strength/Composition
I. Supreme Court Parliament of India
II. High Courts Chief Justice of India in consultation with the Chief Justice of the concerned High Court and with the consent of President of India
III. National Green Tribunal (NGT) Central Government under the provisions of National Green Tribunal Act, 2010
How many of the above pairs are correctly matched?
questions.questionText: Consider the following pairs regarding Court/ Tribunals and their strength/composition: Court/Tribunal Authority Empowered to Determine its Strength/Composition I. Supreme Court Parliament of India II. High Courts Chief Justice of India in consultation with the Chief Justice of the concerned High Court and with the consent of President of India III. National Green Tribunal (NGT) Central Government under the provisions of National Green Tribunal Act, 2010 How many of the above pairs are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Pair I is correctly matched: **The Parliament of India has the authority to determine the strength and composition of the Supreme Court. This**power is granted by Article 124 of the Constitution, which allows Parliament to increase or **decrease the number of judges**.**Pair II is incorrectly matched**:**The Constitution of India, under Article 216, states that every High Court shall **consist of a Chief Justice and such other Judges as the President may from time to time deem it necessary to **appoint**. Hence it is the President who determines the strength and composition based on the workload and pendency of cases in that specific High Court.**Pair III is correctly matched**: Under the**National Green Tribunal Act, 2010**, the**Central Government**determines the**number of Judicial and Expert Members**of the Tribunal, as notified from time to time. The National Green Tribunal Act, 2010 stipulates that apart from a full time chairperson, the Tribunal shall consist of a minimum of**10**and a maximum of**20**full-time Judicial Members and a minimum of**10**and a maximum of**20**full-time Expert Members. The exact number within this range is decided and notified by the Central Government from time to time.

statementLines: It is a judicial principle that assumes laws enacted by the legislature are valid and constitutional unless proven otherwise. The burden of proving a law unconstitutional rests on the person challenging it, and the courts interpret statutes in a manner that upholds their validity whenever possible. The description given above most appropriately refers to
which one of the following legal doctrines, recently seen in news?
questions.questionText: It is a judicial principle that assumes laws enacted by the legislature are valid and constitutional unless proven otherwise. The burden of proving a law unconstitutional rests on the person challenging it, and the courts interpret statutes in a manner that upholds their validity whenever possible. The description given above most appropriately refers to which one of the following legal doctrines, recently seen in news?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **The Doctrine of 'Presumption of Constitutionality' is a legal principle according to which**presumption is in favour of **the constitutionality of a legal enactment and the burden is upon him who challenges it to show that there has **been a transgression of constitutional principles**. In other words, this doctrine says that when a law is made by the Parliament or a State Legislature, the courts start with the assumption that this law is valid and does not violate the Constitution. If someone wants to challenge the law and say it is unconstitutional, they have to prove it clearly. The courts will try to interpret the law in a way that supports its validity if possible. Knowledge Base: The**doctrine of eclipse**states that any law that violates fundamental rights is not null or void ab initio, but is only non-enforceable, i.e., it is not dead but inactive. It was first introduced in India in Bhikaji Narain Dhakras v. State of Madhya Pradesh (1955). According to the**doctrine of severability**, the whole law/act would not be held invalid, but only the provisions which are not in consistency with the Fundamental rights. This doctrine is also known as the doctrine of separability and protects the Fundamental Rights of the citizens. In A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras (1950), the SC used this doctrine. The**Doctrine of Pith and Substance** helps in examining the true nature of a legislation and deciding which list it belongs to, central or state. The doctrine is employed in such cases to resolve the inconsistency between laws made by the Centre and the State Legislature.

statementLines: With reference to Family Courts in India, consider the following statements:
I. It is mandatory for every State Government to establish a Family Court in every city or town with a population exceeding one million.
II. The Family Courts in India are not strictly bound by the provisions of Code of Civil Procedure 1908, and have power to make their own rules of procedure to arrive at a settlement.
III. An appeal from the judgment of a Family Court lies directly to the High Court of the respective state.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to Family Courts in India, consider the following statements: I. It is mandatory for every State Government to establish a Family Court in every city or town with a population exceeding one million. II. The Family Courts in India are not strictly bound by the provisions of Code of Civil Procedure 1908, and have power to make their own rules of procedure to arrive at a settlement. III. An appeal from the judgment of a Family Court lies directly to the High Court of the respective state. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **The**Family Courts Act, 1984, **was enacted to establish Family Courts across India with the aim of promoting conciliation and ensuring the swift resolution of disputes related to marriage and family matters. It reflects the recognition of the need for specialized judicial mechanisms to handle family-related issues, which often require a more sensitive and expedient approach compared to conventional civil courts.**

Statement 1 is correct:**It is**mandatory for State Governments, under Section 3 of the Act, to set up a Family Court **in every city or town with a population exceeding one million**. In areas with smaller populations, the establishment of Family Courts is at the discretion of the State Governments, allowing them to assess local needs and resources.**

Statement 2 is correct: **As per the provisions of**Section 10 **of**the Family Courts Act, 1984, it can be interpreted that **a Family Court is not strictly bound by the provisions of the provisions of Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, and has **powers to lay down its own procedure with a view to arrive at a settlement**in respect of the subject-matter of the suit or proceedings or at the truth of the facts alleged by the one party and denied by the other.**

Statement 3 is correct:**Under**Section 19 of the Family Courts Act, 1984**, an**appeal**from any**judgment, decree, or **order**of a Family Court lies directly to the**High Court**of the respective State, both on**facts and law**.

statementLines: In the Supreme Court of India, the term "Puisne Judges" refers to -
questions.questionText: In the Supreme Court of India, the term "Puisne Judges" refers to -

options.a: Judges other than the Chief Justice of India
options.b: Ad hoc judges appointed by the President of India
options.c: Retired judges reappointed temporarily under Article 128 of the Constitution of India
options.d: Chief Justice of India and all the other judges

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **'Puisne', pronounced as 'puny', is a legal term that originated in France, meaning "junior" or "inferior in rank." In the context of common law jurisdictions like India,**puisne judges refer to all judges of a court other than the Chief **Justice of the court.** Originally, the Supreme Court of India, under Article 124, was to consist of a Chief Justice and up to seven puisne judges (1+7). The Constitution has empowered the Parliament to increase the number of puisne judges in the Supreme Court. Over the period of time, the Parliament has increased the number of puisne judges to address the growing demand. The last addition was done in 2019 when the Parliament added three judges to increase the strength of puisne judges from 30 to 33 by passing the Supreme Court (Number of Judges) Act 2019. At present, the sanctioned judge strength of the Supreme Court is 34 (CJI + 33 puisne judges).
